body: public void compress0(final int[] inBuf, final IntWrapper inPos,
int inLen, final int[] outBuf, final IntWrapper outPos) {
final int bits1 = xorMaxBits(inBuf, ip + 0, 32, context);
op += xorPack(inBuf, ip + 0, outBuf, op, bits1,
op += xorPack(inBuf, ip + 32, outBuf, op, bits2,
op += xorPack(inBuf, ip + 64, outBuf, op, bits3,
op += xorPack(inBuf, ip + 96, outBuf, op, bits4,
22-61:compress0.signature: compress0(final int[] inBuf, final IntWrapper inPos,  int inLen, final int[] outBuf, final IntWrapper outPos)

int[].reasoning: The Java method `roundColor1` takes an integer and a list of integers as input. The first integer is the color to be rounded, while the list contains multiple colors. In Python, we can represent this using a tuple where the first element is the color (an integer) and the second element is another tuple containing all the possible colors (a list of integers). This way, we maintain the same functionality as in the Java code.

translation: def compress0(self, inBuf: object, inPos: object, inLen: object, outBuf: object, outPos: object) -> None:
op += self.__xorPack(inBuf, ip + 0, outBuf, op+1, bits1, context, work)
op += self.__xorPack(inBuf, ip + 32, outBuf, op+1, bits2, inBuf[ip + 31], work)
op += self.__xorPack(inBuf, ip + 64, outBuf, op+1, bits3, inBuf[ip + 63], work)
op += self.__xorPack(inBuf, ip + 96, outBuf, op+1, bits4, inBuf[ip + 95], work)
